ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FAN (Fried rice) used as closest parent; no specific Nasi Paprik entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for Nasi Paprik or its closest parent category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Nasi Paprik as typically served in a Malaysian or Singaporean hawker centre or restaurant, weighing approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Goreng (Fried Rice) — IMR Malaysia MyFCD / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for South East Asia, adapted for Nasi Paprik
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD); FAO/INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No dedicated Nasi Paprik entry exists in MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values are adapted from the closest match: Nasi Goreng (Malaysian fried rice) from MyFCD, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA fried rice entries. Nasi Paprik is a spicier, saucier variant of Malaysian fried rice with added dried chillies, oyster sauce, and kaffir lime leaves; macronutrient profile is comparable to Nasi Goreng with a modest upward adjustment to sodium reflecting the oyster and fish sauce content. All values represent a best-estimate per 100 g of the prepared dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; white rice and fried rice analogous entries
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Nasi Paprik exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 64 is estimated from the closest analogous food: cooked white rice (GI ~64–72, University of Sydney GI Database) and fried rice entries in Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr). Nasi Paprik is a stir-fried rice dish with a comparable carbohydrate matrix to plain fried rice; the addition of chilli, sauce, and protein is not expected to substantially alter the GI relative to the rice base. GI 64 (medium) is used as a conservative estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 82.25) / 100 ≈ 52.6, rounded to 52.9 using 350 g serving × 23.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 82.25 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from oyster sauce, fish sauce, and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Nasi Paprik intake or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a single 350 g serving provides approximately 1470 mg sodium — nearly 64% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from white rice base (GI ~64, GL ~53 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Nasi Paprik in small portions and pair it with additional non-starchy vegetables or protein to blunt the postprandial glucose response, given the high glycemic load of approximately 53 per standard serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium from cooking oil and sauces
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk, Nasi Paprik should be consumed occasionally and in moderate portions, as the combination of sodium (~420 mg/100 g) and saturated fat (~1.4 g/100 g) from cooking oil and sauces contributes to both blood pressure and lipid burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Paprik is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean stir-fried rice dish distinguished by its bold, spicy, and slightly tangy flavour profile derived from dried and fresh chillies, kaffir lime leaves, oyster sauce, fish sauce, and soy sauce. The dish is prepared by stir-frying pre-cooked white rice in a hot wok with aromatics including garlic, onion, and lemongrass, combined with a protein component — most commonly chicken (ayam) or beef (daging) — and a generous amount of chilli-based sauce. The name 'paprik' is derived from the Malay adaptation of 'paprika' or 'pepper', reflecting the dish's defining heat and spice. Nasi Paprik is closely related to Nasi Goreng (Malaysian fried rice) but is distinguished by its saucier consistency and the prominent use of dried chillies and kaffir lime leaves, which impart a fragrant citrus-chilli aroma. Texturally, the rice grains are slightly moist and coated in a reddish-brown sauce, with tender pieces of protein distributed throughout. The dish is commonly served as a single-plate meal in Malaysian and Singaporean hawker centres, mamak stalls, and casual restaurants. Nutritionally, Nasi Paprik is a moderate-energy dish (~168 kcal/100 g) providing a reasonable protein contribution (~6.2 g/100 g) from the meat component, with carbohydrates from white rice (~23.5 g/100 g) forming the dominant macronutrient. Sodium content is notably high (~420 mg/100 g) due to the multi-sauce preparation, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 64 (medium), and the glycemic load per standard 350 g serving is approximately 53, which is clinically significant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is generally halal and dairy-free but contains soy and fish allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Nasi Paprik?
What is the difference between Nasi Paprik and Nasi Goreng?
How much sodium is in Nasi Paprik?
What are the ingredients in Nasi Paprik?

confidence_reason: No dedicated Nasi Paprik entry exists in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest approved source match (Nasi Goreng / Malaysian fried rice) with adjustments for the higher sauce content characteristic of Nasi Paprik. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fried rice entries. Confidence is medium rather than low because the dish is compositionally very similar to the source reference and the adaptation rationale is well-documented.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD Nasi Goreng (fried rice) entry and FAO/INFOODS SEA fried rice data; no dedicated Nasi Paprik entry exists in any approved primary source. Sodium is adjusted upward from the Nasi Goreng baseline to reflect the multi-sauce preparation (oyster sauce, fish sauce, soy sauce) typical of Nasi Paprik. 2. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database white rice and fried rice entries (Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr); no direct GI measurement for Nasi Paprik is available. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR Malaysia MyFCD source database for this food category. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR Malaysia MyFCD source database for this food category. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR Malaysia MyFCD source database for this food category. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in Malaysian fried rice preparations using standard vegetable cooking oil. 7. Chinese local names (辣炒饭 / 辣炒飯) are descriptive translations meaning 'spicy fried rice'; no single standardised Chinese name for Nasi Paprik is established in the literature. 8. Hindi and Tamil names are transliterations of the Malay dish name as used in the Singapore multilingual food context. 9. FoodOn URI FOODON_00002694 and FoodEx2 code A0FAN are used as closest parent matches; no exact ontology code for Nasi Paprik exists in either system.
